name: Charles V Route - Puente del Congosto Loop
name_original: Circular por el Río Tormes por el Puente del Congosto — circular desde La Horcajada
description: Cycle 26.9 miles (43.3 km) along the Río Tormes, passing the historic Puente del Congosto, on this moderate road cycling route.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around La Horcajada traverse the varied landscape of the Ávila province, characterized by river valleys such as the Río Tormes and the Sierra de Castillejos. The region features rolling hills and open countryside, offering a mix of moderate and challenging ascents. Road cyclists can expect routes that navigate through agricultural areas and past historical towns, providing diverse terrain for different skill levels. The elevation changes are consistent with a mountainous environment, with some routes featuring significant climbs. Best road cycling routes around La Horcajada The most popular road cycling route is Valle Río Tormes por la Sierra de Castillejos — circular desde La Horcajada, a 36.9 miles (59.4 km) trail that takes 2 hours 56 minutes to complete, offering views of the river valley and mountain range. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Charles V Route - Puente del Congosto Loop, a moderate 26.9 miles (43.3 km) path. This route follows a historical path, providing scenic views around Puente del Congosto. Local road cyclists also love the Castle of El Barco de Ávila – El Barco de Ávila loop from La Horcajada, a 36.1 miles (58.2 km) trail leading through the historic town of El Barco de Ávila, often completed in about 2 hours 56 minutes. Road cycling around La Horcajada is defined by river valleys, rolling hills, and historical landmarks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more advanced routes with significant elevation gain. The routes in La Horcajada are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore La Horcajada's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How difficult are the road cycling routes around La Horcajada?
A: The road cycling routes around La Horcajada offer a varied landscape with options for different skill levels. While there are 2 easy routes, the majority are moderate (25 routes) or difficult (32 routes), featuring rolling hills and significant elevation changes consistent with a mountainous environment. You can expect a mix of moderate and challenging ascents.

Q: Are there routes suitable for advanced road cyclists in La Horcajada?
A: Yes, La Horcajada is well-suited for advanced road cyclists, with 32 routes classified as difficult. These routes often include significant climbs and cover longer distances. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40308170" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdecorneja Castle – Tormes Bridge loop from Villar de Corneja&lt;/a&gt;, which spans over 72 km with more than 1100 meters of elevation gain.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes near La Horcajada?
A: Yes, several popular road cycling routes in the area are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1316635118/valle-rio-tormes-por-la-sierra-de-castillejos-circular-desde-la-horcajada" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle Río Tormes por la Sierra de Castillejos — circular desde La Horcajada&lt;/a&gt;, offering views of the river valley and mountain range.

Q: What historical landmarks or points of interest can I see while road cycling?
A: The routes often pass through historical towns and near significant landmarks. You can explore sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3287205" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdecorneja Castle&lt;/a&gt;, the historic town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4516184" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Barco de Ávila&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5446009" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente del Congosto&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1316647293/charles-v-route-puente-del-congosto-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charles V Route - Puente del Congosto Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically follows a historical path.

Q: Are there scenic viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: Given the region's characteristics, with river valleys like the Río Tormes and the Sierra de Castillejos, many routes offer scenic views of the natural landscape and surrounding mountains. The varied terrain ensures picturesque vistas throughout your ride.

Q: What are the typical distances of road cycling routes in this region?
A: The road cycling routes around La Horcajada vary in length. Many popular routes are in the range of 40 to 70 kilometers. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38172431" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of El Barco de Ávila – El Barco de Ávila loop from La Horcajada&lt;/a&gt; is about 58 km, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31871350" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Barco de Ávila – Valdecorneja Castle loop from La Horcajada&lt;/a&gt; extends to approximately 63 km.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in La Horcajada?
A: Given the mountainous environment of the Ávila province, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling, with milder temperatures and less extreme weather. Summer can be warm, especially in the valleys, while winter may bring colder temperatures and potential for snow at higher elevations.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in La Horcajada?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. Reviewers often praise the diverse terrain, the mix of river valley and mountain views, and the opportunity to cycle through historical towns and agricultural areas.

Q: Are there any beginner-friendly road cycling routes in La Horcajada?
A: While many routes feature significant elevation, there are 2 routes specifically categorized as easy, which would be more suitable for beginners or those looking for a less strenuous ride. These routes typically have gentler gradients and shorter distances.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling trails in La Horcajada?
A: La Horcajada, being a local town, typically offers public parking options within the town center or near key access points. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival for the most convenient parking locations close to your chosen trailhead.

Q: Are there cafes or places to refuel along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes pass through or near small towns and villages in the region, such as El Barco de Ávila or Puente del Congosto. These settlements often provide opportunities to stop for refreshments, coffee, or a meal, allowing cyclists to refuel during their ride.
tips:
La Horcajada is located in the southwest triangle of the province of Ávila. It is practically the space between the foothills of Gredos and the mountain ranges of Malpartida and Collado del Mirón, on the one hand, and the port of Villatoro and the confluence of the Corneja and Tormes rivers, on the other. This surface has been called "Valdecorneja" for centuries. The Cancionero de Baeza, from 1445, for example, records this. The four most important towns in the Valley are, possibly since Vetton times or before, El Mirón, Piedrahíta, La Horcajada and El Barco de Ávila.

La Horcajada is located about 75 kilometers southwest of the provincial capital, Ávila, about 82 kilometers from Salamanca and 185 kilometers from Madrid. The town is located at 1033 m above sea level. n. m. between the towns of Piedrahíta and Barco de Ávila on the slopes of the Sierra de la Horcajada.

The Salamanca town of Puente del Congosto, located a few kilometers from the entrance of the Tormes river in charro territory, owes its name to the gorges and channels that the erosion of the water has been causing. The origin of the town dates back to the 11th century.

faqs.question: How challenging is the "Circular along the Río Tormes by the Puente del Congosto" route for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate. It covers approximately 43.3 kilometers (26.9 miles) with an elevation gain of about 390 meters (1,280 feet). While it's a road cycling route, a moderate rating suggests good fitness is beneficial, and some sections might present more challenge than a typical flat ride.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: As a road cycling route, the terrain primarily consists of roads. You can expect a mix of quiet country roads. While specific surface quality isn't detailed, routes in the wider Salamanca province can range from smoothly paved surfaces to those with gravel or unpaved sections, especially on routes categorized as moderate.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest or scenic views along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers picturesque views as it traces along the Río Tormes. A significant highlight is the historic Puente del Congosto, a medieval fortified bridge over the river. You'll also pass through La Horcajada, the starting point, and enjoy landscapes of forests, fields, and views of the surrounding mountains in the Valdecorneja region.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this circular cycling route from La Horcajada?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in La Horcajada. While specific parking details are not provided, as a starting point for a circular route, you should be able to find suitable parking within the village or nearby. Consider checking local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty rating, distance of 43.3 km, and elevation gain, this route is generally more suited for cyclists with good fitness and some experience. Beginners or families with young children might find it challenging. It's advisable for less experienced riders to assess their comfort with similar distances and elevation before attempting this route.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle along the Río Tormes by the Puente del Congosto?
faqs.answer: The Valdecorneja region, where this route is located, generally offers pleasant cycling conditions during spring and autumn. These seasons typically provide milder temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summer can be hot, especially in the middle of the day, while winter might bring colder temperatures and potentially less predictable weather.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While dogs are often welcome on many outdoor trails, specific regulations for cycling routes can vary. As this is primarily a road cycling route, dogs would typically need to be safely secured (e.g., in a trailer or basket) if accompanying you. Always ensure your dog's safety and respect local regulations regarding pets in public areas.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: There is no indication that any special permits or fees are required to cycle this route along the Río Tormes or to visit the Puente del Congosto. It is generally accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route or in nearby towns?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can expect to find amenities in the towns along the route. La Horcajada, the starting and ending point, and Puente del Congosto are likely to have cafes, pubs, or restaurants where you can refuel or relax.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other notable trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route partially overlaps with some other paths. Notably, about 41% of the route follows sections of the "Ruta de Carlos V". Smaller portions also intersect with "Ruta del Rio Corneja y Hocino" and "Camino del Cementerio".

faqs.question: What kind of historical significance does the Puente del Congosto hold?
faqs.answer: The Puente del Congosto is a historic medieval fortified bridge over the Río Tormes. It, along with the adjacent Castillo de los Dávila, has been declared a Site of Cultural Interest. Its name, "Puente del Arco Angosto" (Bridge of the Narrow Arch), refers to its distinctive narrow arch. The broader area also has historical connections, with Emperor Carlos V said to have crossed a nearby bridge during his journey to the Yuste monastery.
